D	i	a	m	n	dCALL TRANSCRIPT -JULY 2012	CALL TRANSCRIPT -JULY 2012www.getwsodownload.com

-- 1 of 22 --

1
July 2012 Diamond Call
“Direct Marketing”
with Dan Kennedy and special guest, Rick Cesari
Dan: Good afternoon everybody. Welcome to the Diamond members call. I have as promised with me
today Rick Cesari from the infomercial and direct marketing response world, a world he and I share. I
have recommended his book to you. If you have not already obtained it I'm sure by the end of this call
you will make the decision to do so. And it is, as they say, available at all fine booksellers this very
moment the title of which is Buy Now: Creative Marketing that Gets Customers to Respond to You and
to Your Product.
I've given you in print a more elaborate introduction but I will remind you Rick is the brain or half of the
brain, in some cases integrally involved with some of the biggest brand built by direct response of our
time – the Juice Man, the George Forman Grill, Oxyfresh, Sonic Toothbrush, etc. We'll be talking about
some of those.
I do want to quickly make the point before we get started that I fully recognize that the majority of you
on the call may not be about taking some idea, invention, product, some part of your business and
making it a national or global direct response item. But I would remind you that direct response is direct
response is direct response. And whether you're running a local brick and mortar business, retail
business, service business, professional practice or operating on a broader scale the same principles
apply. In many cases you're selling to the exact same consumers and in many cases you are or should be
making the same kind of message in creative cases that somebody like Rick would make on a global
scale for a giant marketing campaign for a product such as the George Forman Grill.
Many of you, of course, by the way, met George when he was at the Info Summit and probably, I think
it's now, Rick might know, it's either one out of five or one out of four households in America have one
of those puppies. So the odds are you probably see that thing when you walk into your kitchen.
So anyway Rick welcome and appreciate you being on the call and taking the time with us. And I'll start
right out with, I have a handful of questions before we let them ask you questions.
Rick: Sure. First I want to say thank you very much Dan for the opportunity to do this. I really
appreciate it and I've been a big fan of yours. There aren't too many people that go back as far as we
have in this industry and have always been a big fan of yours and your books and your work and I have
to be honest I've used a lot of your ideas and techniques in some of the things I did. And in the
introduction you gave it is very important while some of these products have become very big global
brands, almost all of them started as an idea with very little sales and it was being able to sell to just a
few people and then take the techniques of the infomercial to really magnify that and help them grow.
But at the end of the day the selling techniques and the direct response techniques if they don't work on
a one-on-one or a small business level they won't work on a large level either.
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 2 of 22 --

2
Dan: Yeah and that's a great point. And I do, I'll sort of jump then to what would've been my second
question. So a point in case to all that is what you did with Jay and the Juice Man and so I want to ask
you about that so people really get a handle on how these things start. And as you point out the most
intelligent among us don't start them by blowing a half million dollars on a TV campaign or a radio
campaign or for that matter a print campaign. We sort of proved that we have something we can sell in
some other venue. So give folks a sense of where the Juice Man wound up. And then if you will talk a
little bit about the beginnings on radio with free recipe and the beginnings and the seminar environment.
Rick: Sure. At the end of the time when we started the Juice Man business in 1989 in three and a half
years we grew the sales from zero to $75 million and then we sold it to a company in Chicago called
Sultan and Sultan later was a company that did the George Forman Grill but we'll talk about that later.
But the way I found out about Jay Kordich, I attended a home show in Seattle and if you've ever been to
a small, local trade show there's a lot of booths, 10x10 booths, and people are selling products. Well this
one booth, all the booths had like one or two people around them and this one booth had 50 people
around them. And Jay was there talking about juice extractors and he really had captivated the audience.
And so I asked if I could speak with him after the show and he agreed and we started talking about what
he was doing and he was going around the country at these state fairs, home shows selling anywhere
from one to 20 juicers at a time based on his appearances at these venues.
I had been in the direct response field at the time and thought that we could take this and take what he
was doing on a small level and then build it into something bigger. But it was interesting the way we
started, we didn't go out and make an infomercial right away, to what your comment earlier. Basically
the first thing we did was Jay is a captivating personality and he's a good pitch man. He's good at selling
his product. So what we did was we called different television and TV stations around the country. We
couldn't afford, when we started this, to actually hire a PR firm yet. And there was a company, a
television station we got lucky with in New York. At the time it was WOR and again this goes back to
1989 and to kind of give you an idea Matt Lauer was the local host at the time, who's now, obviously,
well known on Good Morning America.
But anyway Jay appeared on that WOR show. It's funny because it's almost the anniversary coming up.
It was June 30th and he was on a 20 minute segment and basically told people that if they sent us an
envelope with a dollar that he would send them a list of recipes. And so this was right before the July 4th
holiday and we didn't hear anything for a while and I heard from the TV station that their switchboard
was lit up and they had a lot of response. But it was before the internet. So everything happened through
the mail.
And on July 6th the post truck pulls up to our office and the post man gets out and he's got a big, I don't
know if you ever seen the big canvas mail sacks, he has one of those over his shoulder and he comes in
and brings it in and I go, "Wow what's all that?" He goes, "I don't know you got a lot of mail today." He
goes back out to the truck and ends up bringing in three more sacks of envelopes and we ended up
getting over 12,000 responses from that one media appearance that Jay did. And basically what we did is
take all the name and addresses of the people that wrote in and we basically sent them back out a hastily
printed up flyer to sell the juicers and that's what launched the business, that one appearance. But it was
still quite a while before we actually made an infomercial. We used that technique of putting Jay on
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 3 of 22 --

3
radio and TV appearances and having people send us an envelope for free recipes and that's how we
kind of built the base of the business before we actually started doing direct response television. And it's
still something that I use to this day. PR is a very powerful way if you have a good person and it's even
more effective today because you use the PR to drive people to a website and then sell your product.
So if you have a good product, service, spokesperson and a way to get on radio and TV that's a really,
really effective way to either start a business or supplement other types of marketing that you're doing
and it works on both local level and national level.
Dan: You also, if I remember correctly, you also used media and filled seminar rooms.
Rick: Right.
Dan: Right? For the Juice Man?
Rick: Yeah because that was my background. Before getting, that's how I kind of learned the direct
response industry. The first people, and I don't know if it was the first but among the first people to use
infomercials were all the real estate guys – Robert Allen, Albert Lowery – how to buy real estate with no
money down. This was the mid-80s and that was my background. I wasn't real estate before getting into
the direct marketing. And had successfully promoted real estate seminars through direct mail and then
through television. And so before actually selling direct to consumer with the infomercial we basically
made a 30 minute TV show and we would go to a city and we would run the half hour infomercial and
ask people to show up at different ballrooms of local hotels. And we supplemented that with the PR. So
if we went to Houston, for example, we would run local television time, ask people to show up for free
seminars. There'd be a 90 minute lecture about the program. At the end of the time the people could
order juice machines, which we would then ship out of our office in Seattle. And all those seminars were
supplemented with local PR as well.
People are skeptical sometimes or most of the times when they see just advertising. And it's always a
challenge to get people to respond to your ads. When you layer in the PR and the media is talking about
it it adds a level of credibility then that works synergistically with the advertising and the two work
together very well.
Dan: And I would point out to people that we went all the back to kind of the oldest of your case
histories and we're talking 80s and 90s and yet really nothing in terms of the architecture of all of this
has really changed much and there's no reason not to use any and all of these methods in today's world.
Now the internet media gives some additional weapons to integrate with them. But we might very well
take somebody to market this, this very same way today.
Rick: Absolutely. The model itself and still works and the only thing that all the additional, whether it's
social media, whether it's the internet, all those things just give you more marketing tools but the same
basic principles and methods are the same whether it goes back over 25 years with the original Juice
Man to our most current project, which is absolutely internet based. I don't know how many people
listening are familiar with the Go Pro cameras, there the little cameras that you see the skiers and
snowboarders wear on their helmet. That's a company that has used kind of the latest techniques. But to
grow they're on the cover of Inc. Magazine a couple months ago. They grew from zero to 250 million in
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 4 of 22 --

4
under three years. And basically the same marketing model we used 25 years ago just adapted to the
new technology, was responsible for part of that success.
Dan: The second question that I have for you, so you probably, most of the time people come to you
and think they have the next best thing to sliced bread and that they have something really cool and in
some cases, as with the camera, they do.
What I hear more often, which is what I want to ask you about, is from people who think that what their
business is about is really very ordinary and they will struggle mightily trying to find their way to a
unique selling proposition and sometimes insist that there's nothing unique about what they do. They
grumble about being commoditized and price suppressed is a result of being commoditized as well. And
I consistently put examples in front of them of things that couldn't be more mundane and ordinary that
then are made into something more than that. Your business is, of course, largely about that. The camera
is almost an exception to the rule. When you think about a countertop grill or essentially a blender or a
toothbrush. It's hard to be more commoditized in many ways than that and yet you're able to take them
to TV or take them to other media and you're able to build multi-million dollar brands out of them. So I
wonder if you would just talk a little bit about the approach to an ordinary product, toothbrush for
example, and finding a way to present it as something extraordinary.
Rick: Yeah that's a really important point and you talked about the unique selling proposition. We'll
talk more about the camera later because this camera, there's nothing special about this camera except
the mounts and meanwhile this company's growing dramatically and Kodak's going out of business,
going bankrupt.
But go back to answer your question you always have to kind of look at products a different way and I'll
start with the juicer and then I'm going to get into the toothbrush and then the grill because they are, at
the time there were lots of juicers being sold in the country when we started this in 1989. But the
companies that were selling them were appliance manufacturers and the products were being marketed
as appliances, as a kitchen appliance.
What the twist we put on with Jay with the juicer initially was that it wasn't a kitchen appliance, it was a
health device. It was something that would enable you to live longer, to live healthier and so this little
product that you see that's making the fresh juice basically has amazing health benefits and we never
talk about the size of the motor. You always talk about features versus benefits. We never talked about
the blade or the size of the motor or the type of plastic or how much juice it would make. We included
information booklets, the immune strengthening diet, the weight loss juice diet, the anti-aging juice diet.
These are all huge benefits that come out of this little machine. Meanwhile, Krupps and Braun and the
other juicer manufacturers were marketing as a kitchen appliance. And so that's a good example of
finding a unique selling proposition on the juicer.
The toothbrush, the Sonic Care wasn't the first electric toothbrush on the market. I think there was a
product out there called Inter-Plaque. And at the time all of the products out in the marketplace, the most
expensive toothbrush aside from the Inter-Plaque, toothbrushes were I don't know what they cost, a
dollar or $2.00. Sonic Care was $150.00. So the challenge was how are we going to sell $150.00
toothbrush when really nobody understood Sonic technology and what it did. So what we did is really
make a message that was centered around, one, understanding what gum disease was and then how this
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 5 of 22 --

5
product could actually help reverse gum disease. So again we're not talking a lot about the technological
advance as much as we're talking about the benefit to the consumer that they couldn't find somewhere
else. And that's a good example.
With the grill, grills have been around forever, all sorts of different types of grills and there's a funny
story about the George Forman Grill. Sultan, the company that bought our juicer business basically it
was a product that they had on the shelf and it was originally a taco maker and the reason it was slanted
the way it was was that they would put ground beef, put a taco shell underneath and then they had a little
paddle that they would sweep the ground beef into the taco shell. It's funny. And they didn't have any
sales, not surprisingly.
Dan: I didn't know that. I knew the product had been around but I didn't know the taco thing.
Rick: Oh yeah, that's what it was originally and the key to that was twofold, was one attaching it to
George at the time because George is a very likeable fellow but to be honest revealing some behind-the-
scenes secrets George hadn't even used the grill when we made the first infomercial with him. The first
time he walked on the set was the first time he had actually used it. But the key to that was a very simple
thing, was very close up photos of the grease and fat running off of whatever you were cooking so that
we came up with the slogan Knock Out the Fat. And again it had a health benefit. And the other part that
we did was the speed and convenience and that was one of the biggest things was that you could cook
fish or burgers or whatever it was, meal you were cooking in about the half the time because the two
sided cooking. So it's always, there's always, to answer your question Dan there's always a unique way
of looking at a product that features a specific benefit and that's really what you need to do is look at it
from a consumer's viewpoint and what is the benefit or what is the unique benefit that your product or
service can provide to them? And then really go out and find ways of testing that and getting some
consumer response. And then once you do and you hit on it then you can grow something from a small
to very, very large.
Dan: All right, so now let's talk about the camera a little bit since you went there and you got me with
there's no difference in the camera, just the mounts. And obviously apparently a target market.
Rick: Yeah. Well the Go Pro camera is probably even, at this point I'd say it's probably going to surpass
as far as the success the George Forman Grill, as big as the grill is and still is. I think there's over 30
million units sold. Because the camera, and it's interesting because if you take this camera anybody can
go make the camera. The technology is there. What the founder of the company did, a guy named Nick
Woodman, was basically figure out how to mount it with a wristband first, initially and then a chest
strap and then a helmet mount and then eventually a surf board mount so that it tied into all of the
extreme action sports that are out there. And that's how we started marketing it at trade shows.
The other part that tied into this was the current technology and social media, which is Facebook. Our
nickname for the Go Pro camera is the Narsa Cam because people like to take pictures of themselves
and post it on Facebook. And so what we did with the Go Pro camera and what they do very well as a
company is use user generated footage, make zillions of You Tube videos and offer an award for the
best video and it has just taken off through the social media channels amazingly.
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 6 of 22 --

6
What we've helped do is bring to the table the television and this isn't a classic direct response ad where
you see the camera and then there's an 800 number and you can order it. basically everything that these
ads do is user generated footage of people jumping off a cliff or motorcycles or scuba diving or surfing
and there's tag on the beginning that just announces it's a Go Pro, user generated footage and at the end
there's a contest that if you go to their website somebody wins one of everything they make every single
day. So it drives huge amounts of traffic to their website.
Now this is a concept that can be used at any level, at any small level and whether it's with TV or radio
or whatever, as a way of driving traffic to the website and having a giveaway at the end of it is just a
very simple direct response marketing concept. They've just put a little twist on it and have had amazing
success.
Dan: You're also, many people on the call will be familiar with the this exercise regiment is so
difficult it will kill your product, P90X, right? That's like a huge hit.
Rick: Yeah. I wasn't involved in the actual, we place, my company's is Cesari Direct. It's a marketing
agency and we make the commercials and we also buy media time. We place media for the P90X
commercial but we didn't actually make any of the spots for it. But to answer your question, yes that's a
huge, huge success out in the marketplace.
Dan: And of all of these when you think about the entire range of everything you've been involved
with, what do you think the critical elements for a direct response success. When it walks in the door
almost regardless of whether you're going to buy media for it, you're going to produce for it, you're
going to work on it, what peaks your interest or what causes you to shake your head and say, "No hope."
Rick: There's a couple things. I always look for a product and we get, just like you do I'm sure, get lots
and lots of products and people that come to us with inventions and things. And the very first thing I
look for is any amount of sales or consumer response to the product because that means the idea's
already validated. It's much easier to market a product and you had mentioned one earlier Dan, I think
you called it Oxy-Fresh but its Oxy Clean and everyone remembers Billy Mays and the fantastic pitch
guy on TV. But we started working with Oxy Clean when they were doing less than a million dollars in
sales. This was 1996 and it was the Apple family out of Denver, Colorado. And the only way they were
making sales was Billy was on Home Shopping and having some success selling the product there. And
then we were able to take what he was doing on Home Shopping obviously and turn it into a very large
direct response marketing success.
But it goes back to; I don't care if somebody has had success with a product selling on a local, like we
talked earlier, trade shows, state fairs. If there's some consumer response and actual sales that's the first
thing that gets my interest. The second is different categories – cleaning products, like house wares,
anything that helps, different kitchen appliances, if it's something new and unique, exercise and fitness,
beauty and skincare, the area you can call it get rich quick or motivation courses that teach people how
to make money. And we've worked in all these categories and so a couple things is one I look for a
category that has had some traditional success over the years in either direct mail, direct response, on the
internet. And then secondly I look for a product that actually has that validation or consumer feedback.
And those are kind of general perimeters. There's a lot more and there's always exceptions to the rules
but those are two of the biggest things that we look for.
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 7 of 22 --

7
Dan: Well let's go to the specific promise of the book, getting people to buy now. Now when we build a
direct response pitch what are the key elements people ought to keep in mind about media and direct
response?
Rick: Well there's a few things. One, we like to create something that they can't get anywhere else. It's
either a bigger offer, better offer, more enticing offer so that they can't go, like if you have a product at a
retail you can compare it to all the other products on the shelf. So we like to have a unique offer. We like
to build perceived, a lot of perceived value into the product offering. We talked about the juicer. We
included a lot of information, booklets that are very inexpensive to make but they have big promises like
I was talking about the weight loss juice diet, the anti-aging juice diet. So we build a package that has a
lot of perceived value and then really just from a pricing standpoint believe it or not we always like to
try and test and go out at the highest price possible, which as your business grows enables you to
effectively market through different channels.
So when we're building that offer we're basically creating a lot of perceived value and then building it in
a way where it's almost like you keep adding on and you get this and you get this and I know that's kind
of a sometimes but wait there's more kind of mentality. But we found over the years that if done
correctly it entices the consumer to actually pick up the phone.
And Dan you know this being in the business we're in, we don't have a business and why I love this type
of advertising versus what we call conventional or traditional advertising because we can measure how
effectively our ads work because if nobody picks up the phone and nobody goes online you haven't done
a good job. But on the other hand you can take that feedback and make changes to the commercial to
actually get people to respond. And that's the key to everything we do is getting that response.
Dan: So in a few minutes we'll let folks get in and pose questions to you or you and me and let them
play a little bit. Before we do that for those who have not yet followed instructions and gone and gotten
their paws on the book. You want to give them a quick buy now for the book and if you have a website
or anything you want to give out now's the time to do it.
Rick: Okay, I appreciate that. Like Dan had said earlier the book Buy Now is available at any of the
major bookstores or on Amazon. You can order it online and the website for our company is Cesari
Direct, that's C-E-S-A-R-I-D-I-R-E-C-T dot com and it talks a lot about the different products we were
talking about today and a lot of case studies on there and it's just a good source of information following
up on a lot of the things that I talked about today. And kind of the feedback we've been getting on the
book is a lot of inventors are reading it to get ideas on how to market their product. We talk a lot on the
subject matter that Dan you brought up, how to structure offers. It's really a combination of case studies,
stories, a lot of interesting information on these products you've used or heard about along with real
specific how to information. So it's kind of a combination. I've got a lot of feedback to the very
interesting book to read in addition to the information that's in there. So I hope you, if you do get it, that
you read it and enjoy it and feel free to ask any questions then.
Dan: All right Tommy if you want to let people know how to they can get in and play and then we'll
start taking folks who want to get in the game with us.
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 8 of 22 --

8
Tommy: Okay well ladies and gentlemen at this time please press star zero on your phone now to get in
the queue with me for a question. Once again it's star zero if you have a question. And Dan we have our
first question. Sean please go ahead.
Sean: Thanks for the call. My name's Sean Bock and we sell done-for-you marketing systems to
restaurant owners among a few other products. So I know this is B-to-B and we talked a little bit B-to-C
but I know a lot of it crosses over. And I guess my question is is when selling in that environment do
you guys, how do you guys feel, we're trying to craft a direct response campaign for them. Do you guys
feel that crafting lead generation wise or just going straight to a pitch for them is best and if it is lead
generation any ideas on what kind of lead magnet, I guess free report or CD or what seems to work best?
Rick: Dan you want to go ahead?
Dan: I'll go first if you like.
Rick: Sure. Yeah.
Dan: And perhaps some clarification for you. So you guys are providing marketing services that bring
bodies in the door to restaurants of all kinds across the country?
Sean: Correct.
Dan: Correct. In your own marketing efforts to get clients, what are you doing now?
Sean: We'll have the sales people who will go cold basically, which obviously sucks. And then we get a
lot of referrals and then we get a lot of people asking just to do more.
Dan: But you don't have a funnel at this point is what it sounds like?
Sean: No.
Dan: You're still trying to figure that out.
Sean: Yeah.
Dan: Well first of all sort of as a broad comment a formula like Rick described where you don't
necessarily need a unique product but you do need a unique offer. And then the more irresistible the
better in order to now drive leads. And so if you think about your question and you think about your
business situation you really have two things you need to accomplish in general. One, you need to get
restaurant owners to willingly step forward, raise their hand and express interest because we know that
not all restaurant owners are truly interested in an open minded towards new ways of doing business and
getting customers. So you need that to happen.
And then secondly you need a mechanism of converting them to buyers once they do so. In a complex
sale where you're not selling a thing, so you're selling services, not here's a course on how to promote
your restaurant. In a complex sale more often than not my advice would be lead generation, lead capture
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 9 of 22 --

9
and then some sort of presentation. So be that webinar or teleseminar or setting up the appointment for
the boots on the ground to go in if you have that capability or driving them into preview seminars, into
road shows or whatever.
But what I would encourage to really think about related to this call is the bait, is the offer to those
restaurant owners that will bring them to you in the first place that is so well crafted that it attracts the
people that the right kind of attitude to be clients and is irresistible so they have to pay attention and they
have to come forward.
Rick: Dan I would want to add one thing to that, I agree with everything you said, and one of the big
components of anything we do is good testimonials and we can never have a successful product if the
product itself doesn't work or isn't successful and if it is successful and it is a good product you'll
generate happy users. So in this case if you have one really good success story of a restaurant where
you've driven business and they've reaped the rewards of it I would really get, package something in
addition to everything Dan said around a success story and say, "Hey look at what we did for them. We
can do this for you as well." And you can get a lot of mileage out of something like that.
Sean: Well that's what we've been doing, the response hasn't been great so we have some success
stories and we've got one chain that we're getting between 28 and 34% redemption rate on. But it hasn't
been lead generation. It's basically gone straight to hey look at what we've done for them and we can do
the same for you. And we're getting not great response. My question is are you saying after we lead
generate, after we get them interested then we go into the success stories? Okay here's what we do. Or
maybe we need to tweak our success stories and our whole letter and our campaign that we're running
with now to make that work. Does that makes sense?
Dan: Look, it may be all of those things but it sounds to me like you're trying to get across too big of a
canyon in one jump.
Sean: Okay.
Dan: That's what it sounds like to me.
Sean: Okay.
Dan: Back to Rick's point, when you have, and you have to be careful about, see 24% to 36% of those
were the numbers are redemption. That's a real meaningful statement to you, me and to Rick. In fact,
Rick's probably amazed. But to Joe Blow who owns two pizza shops he doesn't have a frame of
reference for that.
Sean: Got ya.
Dan: And so earlier Rick said you got to present this from the consumer perspective. So you've got to
really present this in a way that whoever your target prospect is whether that's a sole proprietor or
somebody that owns five, whoever that is, that they get it. And then you really can't be leaping from first
hand race to signing a contract to have services performed and advertising placed and all that in one big
leap. You're asking for a bigger commitment then buying a book or a home study course or a project.
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 10 of 22 --

10
As to where and when you use testimonials I think you probably use them at every stage of the game
both to get a hand raised without specificity and then to demonstrate the efficacy of what you do with
specificity. Have you done any B-to-B, Rick? [Inaudible 38:01] is B-to-B obviously.
Rick: Yeah when we develop new business we have to do B-to-B marketing and really what we do is,
and I think that what you said there's a middle step, and again we're not working, I would need to know
all the information, all the details to really come up with a real specific answer that's going to actually
help you out. But we do B-to-B marketing for our own business and that's the way we do it is really lead
with case studies and success stories. But then after somebody, that's a lead gen. After somebody comes
in the door we have a whole package of information that talks about the specific things we're going to
do, the benefits of the customer, the step-by-step and before we actually sit down and talk to that person.
And for us, because we're selling something when somebody hires our company they can be spending
anywhere from two to $500,000.00 to get involved, that there are a lot of steps in between before the
actual contract is signed. But we get the initial lead.
Dan had asked me about how the book was doing and it's doing well but it's really good for new
business development and that was one of the reasons we wrote the book was to get out on PR around
the country, people would hear us, they would contact our company, we would evaluate the product.
Then we have a whole bunch of stages before we actually have that face-to-face meeting and close the
deal. So there's many stages to getting to closing a large B-to-B sale like that. But it all starts with the
contact and the lead gen and then following-up with the correct support materials before the actual
closing of the deal.
Sean: And that's what we're going after is the larger, more larger restaurant, less moms and pops.
Dan: Well you ain't going to get there in one big jump.
Rick: Yeah that's the other thing I hear all the time, someone wants to say, they have a great product,
"Oh I want to put it on Oprah." I know that show isn't on the air anymore. And they forget that the way
you get to Oprah is you do a little radio show, then you do a bigger radio show and then you do a local
TV show, then you do a national show and after you have all that success maybe if you're lucky you'll
get on a show like Oprah. I know your target is the big national chains but the more little success stories
and building up and create that solid base to work from the more success you're going to have with the
bigger guys.
Dan: All right we're going to have to move on. Take our next question. Thank you.
Tommy: Okay, our next question's from Brody. Please go ahead.
Brody: Hey that's me. My name's Brody Tyler. Can you hear me okay?
Dan: Yep we can hear you.
Brody: All right. I have so many questions but I will pick one. I know you got George Forman to
endorse the grill. And it seemed like it was nothing until he put his name on it literally. What does it take
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 11 of 22 --

11
to get a celebrity endorsement whether it's for a product, service or a foreword for a book that's been
written? How do you approach that? Is it profit sharing going on? What are the details of that
arrangement?
Rick: Yeah it's a great question and I do a lot of this on every different level. The George Forman I
think until recently there was a good answer to a trivia question – what was the largest sports
endorsement ever? And it was really what George paid, got paid when they bought him out of the
George Forman Grill or bought his royalty stream out or profit sharing out. And he got paid $130
million dollars for that.
But when they did the deal basically they didn't pay George anything. It was a situation where George
had, after he lost that fight in Zaire to Muhammad Ali he had been through like a 10 year stretch where
he just got fat and out of shape and he was making a comeback and he didn't have very many
endorsements because his reputation in the old days in this, he says this in his own book, he was kind of
a thug. Nobody really wanted to do endorsements. So he was an easy guy to approach to make a deal
and there was no money up front that changed hands. So they signed George Forman for literally
nothing but they did a profit sharing deal. And obviously it worked out very well for George. So that's
one thing to keep on your radar.
When we look for people to represent certain infomercials or be host and things like that I always look
for people that had had a lot of fame at one time but maybe are on the downward side of their career.
But people still have fond memories of them and things like that. Those are people that can be signed a
little bit cheaper. And there's all sorts of ways of doing it.
A profit sharing way is one where it doesn't cost a lot of money up front but you could be paying a lot of
money if your product is real successful. But it's better to have that success and pay that big fee than not
have any success at all. Then there's other ways with celebrities that just won't talk to you unless there's
cash up front and a lot of it is a combination of cash and royalties from sales.
The other thing when a lot of the times we make these shows we get a lot of experts that whether they're
medical doctors or different book authors and we just approach them and, you ask how do you get these
people, I just always pick up the phone and try and track them down and put in a phone call because any
exposure, if they're involved in your project and it's a good product, they're going to get exposure and
that helps everybody. If somebody's a book author they want to be in our infomercials for free. We never
pay anybody to be in those, any of the experts we use because they want the exposure. So there's all
sorts of different ways of structuring the deals. It's just limited to the creativity and don't assume that all
these people want a lot of money. They might but there's ways of structuring it whether you have to pay
very little money up front and it's based on the success of the project that they can make it big.
Brody: The last thing is what's in it for them is the exposure?
Rick: Yeah.
Dan: Well in some cases if you have, so like for a client some years ago I got Art Linkletter and I got
Art at a lower price than we should have but the client marketed to seniors. Art had a new book out with
Mark Victor Hansen aimed at that market. And so giving away books in all the outlets, promoting the
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 12 of 22 --

12
book in all the ads was the same as currency and it allowed us to get a much lower fee arrangement with
Art. So Rick's point about creativity. A point I always make to people is when you set out to go down
this path you should never set out with locked in on a person but more a category that will be useful to
you. So it might be an athlete, might be a sitcom mom, might be because on any one given day two of
them that are almost interchangeable, one will have just come out of good paying gig and will be
quoting some astronomical fee and won't want to talk to you. And the other one hasn't had a good
paying gig in five years and has a kid's college tuition payment due. And they're a lot more malleable.
The highest rate of unemployment in any occupation in America is entertainment. And so generally
there is somebody for every need and every budget who can be found and had and be useful. And also in
many cases a client will start with let's say somebody from the D list and have enough success that two
years later they can move up to somebody on the B list. At Guthy Renker we launched ProActiv™.
ProActiv™ today there's Justin Bieber and Katy Perry and the youngest Kardashian, whatever her name
is.
Rick: Yeah that's a great point. They didn't start there though.
Dan: We started with Judith Light.
Rick: Yeah who's that?
Dan: The sitcom mom for Who's the Boss after Who's the Boss had been off the air for five years. So
there's almost always somebody and in many cases you may have other valued offer besides money.
Now the mechanics of getting to them, the first best way is always the back door and so I tell people all
the time to really think about the Kevin Bacon game. You are usually no more than three to five
connections away from somebody who would be useful to you. So you got to rack your brain about who
you know and they might know and who they might know because a direct phone call saying, "Hey
Kennedy's a good guy and he'll shoot straight with you. And he wants to talk to you about X," is
infinitely better than the front door. But there are front doors; there are celebrity brokers who represent
buyers, kind of like buyers agents in real estate. And all of that now is more than we're going to take
time for on this call. But if you are really sort of serious about pursuing this and you fax me at my office
or get through to customer service at GKIC I'll be happy to send you some more information that would
be helpful to you.
Brody: Well thank you. I appreciate that.
Dan: I want to be mindful of the clock and Rick's time.
Rick: Yeah I have to jump off in about nine minutes.
Dan: We're going to lose you in nine. They're going to keep me for another 20.
Rick: Okay.
Dan: And so do we have another one Tommy?
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 13 of 22 --

13
Tommy: Yes. Our next question is from Dwight. Please go ahead.
Dwight: So I'm going to assume that it's still a good idea to have your own TV show for your local
business. So Mr. Cesari what would be the key elements to have on, let's say if it was a weekly or even
bi-weekly TV show for a local business. Success stories I know is one but should it always be successful
stories or what other elements could we put in there?
Rick: When you say your own local TV show do you have a specific business that you're talking about?
Because when I talk about TV shows aren't ours regular television shows. Ours are direct response
commercials and they're either 30 minute commercials or one minute sponsor.
Dwight: I should've clarified. I'm actually talking about an internet TV show.
Rick: Oh okay. Obviously, and again this is an area that I have a lot of clients getting into and we do
some as well. It has to be really the category, and again I'm just picking something out of thin air,
whether it's fishing or whatever.
Dan: This is actually martial arts if that helps.
Rick: Oh okay. Well then to me the more guests you can have use what works on regular TV. If you
see something on regular TV, if it's in the martial arts area I would try to get as many interviews and
people that can come on to your show that can be bring something, information outside of what you
provide on a regular basis. And it's nice to maybe create the show in a way where there's recurring
segments, sort of like a newsletter format or an editorial format for a magazine. There's a segment,
obviously if it's your show featuring something that you do and a tip of the week. You might have a new
guest on every week. You might have a success story. You might have a kid that's taking martial arts
and why this is great for kids because it teaches them discipline and respect. Think up different recurring
categories and then the challenge is just making sure that you get new and fresh information each and
every week or day that you do the show.
Dwight: And Dan I just found out that my Kevin Bacon is two.
Dan: Oh is that how far away you are?
Dwight: Yep.
Dan: From him or from someone you want?
Dwight: From him.
Dan: Oh well, there you go. What is that a client, a student?
Dwight: No actually I was in a movie with a guy who was in a movie with him.
Dan: Well there you go. See? Unfortunately that's probably not really useful to you but I'll keep in
mind. You never know when I might want to get to Kevin Bacon. And now I know I'm just three away.

Dwight: Ah.
Dan: See?
Rick: Good point.
Dan: All right Dwight, thanks. Let's try and squeeze in one more real quick.
Tommy: Next question we have is from Bob. Please go ahead.
Bob: Hi Dan, Rick. Thank you very much. My question changed midway through. I came in a little
late. It's with the buy now versus lead generation. I'm an NLP guy and we've been selling NLP training
online successfully for three years. Had a little snag this year because people come to our site. It
surprised us how well it worked. People come to the website, see that we offer NLP training online, buy
it. Or they get to, if they don't buy it right away they get a well written Dan Kennedy style sequence that
goes out with sales pitch letter starting, within 14 days they buy it. That was doing about 25% of the
people coming in and getting people in, obviously, is what we were struggling with.
Now everyone says, "Hey got to do lead generation." So we did a big thing in the spring to change over
to lead generation. Apparently I don't know how to do that because we stopped getting income. So is
there a trick that I don't know that has a shortcut in the process because we can't stop people from, we
don't want to stop people from buying now when they come in but lead generation on that front end now
is interrupting the buy now process.
Dan: Why on earth did you stop doing something that was converting a 25%?
Bob: Can I charge you for questions or…? Because we wanted more people and I figured okay we're
only getting so many people coming in that's pretty good but we wanted to increase the income so I
thought maybe we can somehow convert the ones that are getting away.
Dan: Okay. Rick you want to play?
Rick: Well when you talked about the title of the book Buy Now it's a little bit of a misnomer because
yes we want people to buy now but if we're doing a lead gen the whole purpose of direct response is
getting people to take an action and the lead gen is a huge part of our business. Sometimes it's the two-
step buy now. So all were trying to do with our advertising thing in any program we do is whether it's
lead gen or advertising they're either purchasing now or they're responding to our message and then
making the purchase secondary.
Again my first reaction was the same as yours Dan, it sounded like there was a model that was working
very well and then you changed it and what you changed it to isn't working. So I don't know, I would go
back to what was working and then don't make such a drastic change. Again without knowing more of
the specifics it's hard to give a really good answer to that.
Bob: Right. I caught on to the go back to what we were doing after a couple of months.
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 15 of 22 --

15
Dan: Yeah. What you should be setting up is…so 25% convert with that process. 75% obviously do not
convert with that process, which by the way is an enviable, I'm sure Rick would agree, online
conversion percentage. So the real question now is not to get in the way of the 25% but what we can do
with the 75% that is more effective after the first yes/no? Because they have real value.
Now if even asking them is driving away huge numbers then you are dividing by 25 and 75 then you
really, you want two separate marketing campaigns driving into two separate places that function
differently. But you damn sure do not want to get in the way of the 25% who are willing to say yes.
Rick: Yeah, and I would spend a lot more time figuring out how to drive more people into that funnel
because it's working and spending time basically figuring out different ways to drive more people into
the closing process that's already working.
Bob: Right. Okay. So there's no magic. That was just like okay.
Dan: No you haven't, see I agree with what Rick just said, you get to a winner in pieces. So you have to
conquer how can I get lead flow at an affordable cost? How can I convert leads? How can I monetize the
unconverted leads? In some cases not yours. How can I move from direct response to forced retail
distribution, which is something that Rick's very good at. So you get there in pieces. So you now have a
piece that works. You got a funnel that works and it converts.
So long before I'd be monkeying around with that I'd be trying to get the other pieces up to par, which
the best piece to get up to par now is how can we drive 10 times more traffic?
Bob: Right, right.
Dan: That’s the game here. All right I got to cut you off and I think have to let Rick go. Thank you very
much, sir, for your time and participation.
Rick: Great. I really appreciate the opportunity. This is fun stuff to do.
Dan: And again your website?
Rick: Is CesariDirect.com, C-E-S-A-R-I-D-I-R-E-C-T dot com. And then the book Buy Now you can
get at any bookstore or through Amazon.

Dan: Hey Rick.
Rick: Hey Dan how are you?
Dan: I'm good.
Rick: Good. Ricky Ratchet. I live in Ontario, Canada, The Auto Repair Guy.
Dan: Yes.
Rick: I have bought that book, read the book and couple things have happened recently that kind of
made sense with this book reading it this time. We did a direct mail with a really good offer, a lot of
offers to check a lot of things on people's cars – change their oil, do a series of test. It was a really well
laid out letter. It didn't perform. The sales letter didn't work. We got offered by Groupon to do an offer
through them. So we put the same offer with them. They did a lousy job of writing it up and it
performed well. A lot of people bought that and it has placed cars in our shop and people bought. And
they actually got a list of what was wrong with their car, got some time spent, which they bought the
Groupon for and they saw good value in it and then in turn made a purchase.
After reading the book I thought, and I'm regularly on television. I'm on during the Jay Leno, 11:00
News, nighttime television but it's costing me around $650.00 to be on that spot and we're branding and
we're offering a free oil change on that. There's nothing near as big as what the Groupon was.
I've recently looked into 60 second spots during the day and thought I might do a Billy Mays style
commercial putting this offer together live and I was just looking for any tips on how I might do that. If
I'm on the right track. And furthermore just by listening here today I would think that driving them to a
website or just driving them to phone would be the way to go. Any tips?
Dan: Well first thing I will say is that not all Groupon experiences are as happy as yours. So
congratulations.
Bob: Well I planned that. I'll tell you how in a minute.
Dan: Yeah, because you clearly had a very good sales and conversion process in place.
Bob: Yes.
Dan: Which a lot of people don't and they get brutalized as a result. But what it tells you is is that the
people to who the offer is directed matter a lot. And so you have value shoppers to whom you put a great
value in front of. Whereas your direct mail lots of ways for misfire, of course, but one is that that's a
different audience. And so the heavy value offer may in fact actually work against you rather than for
you in a colder, broader, fuzzier list or environment.
And so you may have gone so far, for example as to have raised skepticism and harm trust. You may
have de-emphasized other reasons to choose you versus other options other than the value offer. So who
we get in front of with each message matters a lot.
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 17 of 22 --

17
Specific to your TV question I absolutely would be driving to phone and web. I think it's too early to
drive just to web.
Bob: Okay.
Dan: But the more you can drive to web the better because you can do more there with them.
Bob: I can do my testimonials there and I can…
Dan: You got it.
Bob: I can keep them there.
Dan: Yeah. So the order in which you present the response instruction now is web first, phone second
and ideally you create some kind of instant gratification incentive, a reason to go to the web that is not
connected to the phone, which may very well be and go to the web and see a four and half minute video
about X or call and obviously you can't see the video if you don't go to the web because you want to
weight the traffic there if you can.
Now since, I'll let you work, so for everybody's benefit tell me about your conversion process in place
for your Groupon traffic.
Bob: Okay. I've been reading about Groupon. I've been listening to the calls. I've been going on You
Tube and looking at Groupon damage and assessing that, I simply put in my Groupon must have
appointment. And secondly I just increased the offer so much that it takes a whole day for the car to stay
here to get it. So I could actually control the flow and I just made it all about controlling how many
would come and making it more a value for them if they leave the car for the entire day. And quite
honestly if the car's here for the entire day there's a better chance I got a chance to sell something.
Dan: Yep.
Bob: So I played with the free oil change before and that's a toe tapping every 20 minutes somebody's
wanting to get out of your shop and get going and get what they got for free. I thought if I increase the
offer and control the flow I can do a better job and increase the value for them. And it worked really
through Groupon.
Dan: And they paid for that whole day long inspection?
Bob: Yes. Yes. They paid Groupon and then I got a share of that.
Dan: Yeah, yeah, yeah, yeah, yeah, okay.
Bob: In the direct mail package we didn't even put a price on it. We offered 17 free and we didn't get
any response.

Tommy: Okay next question we have is from Dwight Montgomery. Please go ahead.
Dwight: Hey Dan. How you doing this afternoon.
Dan: Hey. Real good, you?
Dwight: Super thanks. Hey thanks a lot for organizing Rick. That was very informative. I've got two
questions. I have heeded your advice and I refuse to commoditize in the proverbial tomato, although our
industry, I'm in the office furniture industry. All you have to do is walk into Staples or any of the box
stores and that's what you find. So what I've set out to do is to go the other direction and create a chair
that is designed specifically for overweight Americans. I'm looking at trends and I'm seeing the
McDonald's and Krispy Kreme aren't going anywhere and that the obesity rate is over the top. And what
I'm suggesting is that there are chairs that are not made with cheap plastic. There are chairs that can be
produced with metal and they're not going to sink to the floor when sat upon.
So I'm looking at it from a trend basis, A. But I guess my two questions are as follows, and I've been in
the office furniture industry successfully for 18 years. So I have a track record. We probably eight or
$900,000.00 worth of chairs on annualized basis but not this chair, this particular prototype that I'm
working on. So with that as the context and the back drop I'm wondering do you have a source that you
would recommend in your general overview thinking of what a business plan surrounding that would
encompass. The direct response market is where I think this has play but that's A typical from the typical
business plan and planners that I've worked with. So do you have a resource to work with in regard?
And then B, I had a great conversation with Fred Catone who said, because I was asking him what made
him different? What differentiated that product launch? He said, "That was simple." He said, "I gave
people a choice." So as I pondered on that I was like, you know what? I may be missing the real boat
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 19 of 22 --

19
here, which is why not create a chair that gives people a choice because right now your choice is you go
sit down and does it feel right or not? But there are health concerns. If you're 6'4" and the next guy's 5'8"
and the same chair is designed to sit on the back of their leg it cuts off circulation. There's a bunch of
research that's out there to support that. So I guess my question to you is, A, do you have a group that
you would recommend working with as far as business planning is concerned? B, what are your initial
gut response to do you go after BigAssChairs.com or do you go after, you chose your chair based upon
very similar to a custom suit. I'm 6'2". My inseam is X and I weigh 209 pounds.
Dan: From a marketing standpoint I'm not sure one precludes the other. But I am sort of knee jerk
reacted to the idea of customization. What's you inseam? What's your weight? That's interesting. There
are plus size furniture companies. There's a very high end one that advertises just Western Décor
Furniture that's all plus sizes. There are plus size bed manufacturers. And so you're not the first to
identify the need, the trend, the customer. You may very well be the first one in office chairs. I certainly
have not heard about it.
You are well aware that there are fairly pricey ergonomic and adjustable chairs now sold and sold by
direct response. There's one that advertises in the New Yorker Magazine every month that is a lead
generation ad and there's a website and it's a fairly expensive chair.
So I think there is a direct marketing play here for you, assuming you have the right margins. There's a
direct marketing play here for you. I think there's still a regular distribution play here for you as well. As
to refer to you no I don't. But I would tell you, so part of my approach if I were to be in your position or
to begin working on your behalf is I would go look for comparables, for business models now of a
successes in customized anything, in stuff for overweight people anything, in back comfort seating
anything. Cesari has a client called Back Joy Orthotics. I did a little bit of consulting with them early.
And they're a company worth looking at. The real high priced ergonomic chairs and I can't for the life of
me think of the name of it but it's in the New Yorker Magazine every month and somebody got me one
as a gift. So I happen to have one in my office.
So I would find existent businesses that are doing a lot of the things I would need to do, are in a
comparable category, are selling to the same consumer, are selling some item at a much higher price
than the average in the industry because it is whiz bang and special. So I would find my five or six
models to really build my business strategy backwards from.
Dwight: Okay.
Dan: That's where I'd start and that's where I'd tell you to start.
Dwight: So what's your knee jerk reaction as to why that particular furniture manufacturer that's
advertising in the New Yorker, that I'm not seeing any infomercials, any George Forman's, any William
'The Refrigerator' Perry type of…
Dan: Because it's viewed still as a business purchase rather than a consumer purchase, which you know
the plethora of home offices…I mean the very fact that he's in the New Yorker and he's not in Business
Week tells you he's drifted in that direction. But I would imagine most people look at it and view it as
too narrow. You know when you really broadcast, so a TV infomercial, a mainstream radio spot, now I
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 20 of 22 --

20
think you could do radio but you'd have to be selective. Limbaugh has the highest percentage of
business owner listeners of anything on the air. Could you do Limbaugh? Could you do Beck? Maybe.
Could you do broad based radio just buying main radio buys? No. I don't think so.
So it would probably be looked at as too narrow. Now the plethora of home offices would suggest
maybe otherwise and the fact that nobody has tried it doesn't mean that it can't be done. It just is a
product nobody has decided to put the muscle behind and try and test on a big mainstream basis. But the
very fact that they can be with an ergonomic office chair in the New Yorker, which is an affluent skew
publication but not necessarily a businesses skew publication. It says a lot. And suggests that that play
may very well exist. All right?
Dwight: Super. Thanks for the information Dan.
